topic: Speech, Structure, Comprehension, Vocabulary, Continuous Writing

subUnits.title: 9.1 Speech: Listening to Identify a Speakers Style
notes: Listening to Identify a Speakers Style:
2. Students can read and others identify the speakers style.
1. Teacher guides students to understand style
- Descriptive: paints a picture with words
- Argumentative: persuades with logic and evidence
diagramDescriptions: Chart of speaking style types with examples
Diagram of style features to identify
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English listening speakers style identification SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Expository style primarily aims to:
quiz.explanation: Expository style explains or informs using facts, definitions, and logical organization.

quiz.q: Narrative style is characterized by:
opts: Telling a story with characters and plot
quiz.explanation: Narrative style tells a story, often with characters, setting, and plot development.

quiz.q: Descriptive style uses:
quiz.explanation: Descriptive style uses sensory details and vivid language to create mental images.

quiz.q: Argumentative style relies on:
quiz.explanation: Argumentative style uses logic, evidence, and reasoning to persuade the audience.

quiz.q: To identify a speakers style, you should look at:
opts: Word choice, sentence structure, and tone
quiz.explanation: Style is identified through word choice, sentence structure, tone, and organization.

quiz.q: Formal style is characterized by:
quiz.explanation: Formal style uses precise vocabulary, complex sentences, and avoids colloquialisms.

quiz.q: Figurative language is most common in:
quiz.explanation: Figurative language (metaphor, simile) is common in descriptive and narrative styles.

quiz.q: Understanding speaking styles is important because it:
opts: Supports critical listening and effective communication
quiz.explanation: Recognizing styles supports critical listening, comprehension, and effective communication.

quiz.q: Which is NOT a feature to analyze in style?
quiz.explanation: Font size is a visual feature, not a linguistic feature of speaking style.

quiz.q: A common misconception about style is:
opts: Style can be identified through analysis
Only one style is correct for all purposes
Style affects how messages are received
quiz.explanation: Different purposes and audiences require different styles; no single style is universally best.

subUnits.title: 9.2 Structure: Introduction to Active and Passive Voice
notes: Introduction to Active and Passive Voice:
Example: A rat was killed by Ojo
1. Teacher guides students to give more examples
2. Use the appropriate form of be + past participle
- When the doer is unknown or unimportant
- When the focus is on the action or recipient
- Using passive when active is clearer
- Incorrect verb form in passive construction
- Omitting necessary by-phrase when meaning is unclear
diagramDescriptions: Chart comparing active and passive voice structures
Diagram of active to passive conversion steps
Flowchart for choosing active or passive voice
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English grammar active passive voice SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: In active voice, the subject:
quiz.explanation: In active voice, the subject performs the action of the verb.

quiz.q: The passive form of The boy kicked the ball is:
opts: The ball was kicked by the boy.
The boy was kicked by the ball.
quiz.explanation: In passive voice, the object becomes subject: The ball was kicked by the boy.

quiz.q: Passive voice is formed with:
opts: Object + be + past participle + by + subject
quiz.explanation: Passive voice uses: object + be verb + past participle + by + original subject.

quiz.q: Which sentence is in passive voice?
opts: The lesson was explained by the teacher.
The teacher is explaining the lesson.
quiz.explanation: The lesson was explained by the teacher uses passive voice structure.

quiz.q: Passive voice is appropriate when:
opts: The doer is important to mention
The focus is on the action or recipient
You want to be more direct
quiz.explanation: Passive voice is appropriate when the focus is on the action or recipient, not the doer.

quiz.q: The past participle of write is:
quiz.explanation: Written is the past participle of write, used in passive constructions.

quiz.q: Which is a common error with passive voice?
opts: Using passive when active is clearer
quiz.explanation: Using passive when active voice would be clearer is a common error.

quiz.q: Understanding active/passive voice is important because it:
quiz.explanation: Knowing when to use active or passive voice supports clear, appropriate communication.

quiz.q: Which sentence is in active voice?
opts: The cake was eaten by the children.
Eaten was the cake by the children.
quiz.explanation: The children ate the cake has subject performing action: active voice.

quiz.q: A common misconception about passive voice is:
opts: It has appropriate uses in formal writing
quiz.explanation: Passive voice is not always wrong; it has appropriate uses in formal or scientific writing.

subUnits.title: 9.3 Comprehension: Reading to Answer Questions
notes: 2. Key words in the passage listed
1. Teacher guides the students as they answer questions
2. Ensure students follow instructions on number of answers and time allowed
2. Identify the question type (literal, inferential, evaluative)
3. Locate relevant information in the text
4. Formulate your answer in your own words
5. Review your answer for completeness and accuracy
- Literal: answer directly stated in text
- Inferential: answer requires reading between lines
- Evaluative: answer requires judgment or opinion
- Applied: answer requires applying information to new situation
diagramDescriptions: Chart of question types with examples
Sample passage with highlighted key information
Comparison of question types and strategies
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English reading comprehension answering questions SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Literal questions require answers that are:
quiz.explanation: Literal questions have answers directly stated in the text.

quiz.q: Inferential questions require the reader to:
opts: Use logical reasoning based on context
quiz.explanation: Inferential questions require reading between the lines using context and reasoning.

quiz.q: The first step in answering comprehension questions is to:
quiz.explanation: Reading the passage carefully is essential before attempting to answer questions.

quiz.q: Evaluative questions require the reader to:
opts: Make a judgment supported by textual evidence
quiz.explanation: Evaluative questions require judgment or opinion, supported by evidence from the text.

quiz.q: To locate information for answering questions, you should:
opts: Scan for keywords from the question
quiz.explanation: Scanning for keywords from the question helps locate relevant information efficiently.

quiz.q: A common error in answering comprehension questions is:
opts: Using your own words for inferential questions
Copying text verbatim for all question types
quiz.explanation: Copying text verbatim is appropriate only for literal questions; other types require original thought.

quiz.q: Applied questions require the reader to:
opts: Apply text information to new situations
quiz.explanation: Applied questions require connecting text ideas to real-world scenarios or new situations.

quiz.q: Understanding comprehension strategies is important because it:
opts: Supports critical thinking and effective communication
quiz.explanation: Comprehension strategies support critical thinking, media literacy, and informed citizenship.

quiz.q: Which is NOT a type of comprehension question?
quiz.explanation: Spelling is not a comprehension question type; literal, inferential, evaluative, and applied are.

quiz.q: A common misconception about comprehension is:
opts: Answers must be concise and relevant
There is always only one correct answer
Context clues help with inferential questions
quiz.explanation: Some comprehension questions allow for multiple valid interpretations; not all have a single correct answer.

subUnits.title: 9.4 Vocabulary Development: British and American Spellings
notes: British and American Spellings of Common Words:
- Choose one spelling convention and stick to it
- Be aware of your audience and context
- Use dictionary to verify uncertain spellings
- Note that some words have accepted variants
diagramDescriptions: Chart comparing British and American spellings
Table of common spelling differences with examples
Sample text showing both spelling conventions
Flowchart for choosing appropriate spelling convention
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English British American spelling differences SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The British spelling of color is:
quiz.explanation: British English uses our ending: colour, while American uses or: color.

quiz.q: The American spelling of centre is:
quiz.explanation: American English uses er ending: center, while British uses re: centre.

quiz.q: Which pair shows correct British/American spelling?
quiz.explanation: Theatre is British; theater is American.

quiz.q: The British spelling of defense is:
quiz.explanation: British English uses ce: defence; American uses se: defense.

quiz.q: Which word has the same spelling in both British and American English?
quiz.explanation: Program is spelled the same in both, though British English uses programme for broadcast.

quiz.q: The American spelling of travelling is:
quiz.explanation: American English typically uses one l: traveling; British uses double l: travelling.

quiz.q: When writing for an international audience, you should:
opts: Choose one convention and be consistent
quiz.explanation: Consistency within one convention is more important than which convention you choose.

quiz.q: Understanding spelling differences is important because it:
opts: Supports clear communication in global contexts
quiz.explanation: Awareness of spelling conventions supports clear, professional communication in diverse contexts.

quiz.q: Which is a tip for consistent spelling?
opts: Choose one convention and stick to it
Use both conventions in the same document
quiz.explanation: Choosing and sticking to one spelling convention ensures consistency and professionalism.

quiz.q: A common error with British/American spelling is:
opts: Mixing conventions within the same document
quiz.explanation: Mixing British and American spellings in the same document appears unprofessional and inconsistent.
